Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Deaf Woman Says Disability Caused Flight Removal, But Frontier Airlines Says She Had Alcohol

Viral Frontier removal dispute pits disability-accommodation claims against the airline’s alcohol accusation.

Airlines face mounting pressure to document disability vs. misconduct incidents thoroughly, as social media amplifies disputes that could influence regulatory scrutiny of accommodation practices.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

The 5 Longest US Domestic Flights In 2026

All five start or end in Hawaii.

Hawaii's geographic isolation creates five of the longest domestic routes in the US network, presenting unique operational and scheduling challenges for carriers serving the islands.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Sitting Comfortably? The Top 10 Busiest US Regional Jet Airports

Chicago O'Hare reigns supreme as the busiest airport for regional flights in the US, with over 128,000 takeoffs and landings in Q2 2026.

Regional jet hubs concentrate traffic at major metros, creating capacity constraints that pressurize feeder network economics and gate availability for carriers.

See Simple Flying →

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards program directly influences aerospace contractor sustainability standards and compliance requirements across the industry supply chain.

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Safety Management Systems succeed only when organizations create psychological safety that encourages frontline workers to report hazards without fear of punishment.

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen engine components risk circumventing traceability systems, potentially compromising fleet safety across European operators if counterfeit parts integrate into supply chains.

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Inside The Airbus A350's Avionics Bay

The aircraft is one of the world's most advanced machines.

The A350's avionics integration demonstrates how modern aircraft consolidate complex systems into modular designs, reducing maintenance costs and improving dispatch reliability.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

Astrolab and Zeno Power's membership signals growing industry consolidation around lunar surface mobility and power systems critical for sustained commercial space operations.

See SpaceNews →

How to Assess Safety Risk

A practical walkthrough of safety risk assessment under 14 CFR Part 5.

Part 5 risk assessment frameworks enable operators to systematically quantify safety hazards and justify operational decisions with data-driven evidence rather than assumption.

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

Put Them On Blast: TMZ Asks Public For Photos Of Vacationing Congress Members Amid TSA Crisis

The shutdown, now in its 41st day, has left around 50,000 TSA officers without a paycheck.

The TSA staffing crisis from unpaid officers creates operational vulnerabilities that could affect flight schedules and security protocols across commercial aviation networks.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

Europe's delayed Gateway decision could fragment lunar exploration partnerships if ESA commits resources to a NASA-deprioritized program without clear integration into revised Artemis timelines.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

American Airlines Strongly Considers Bringing Back Seatback Screens, Eyes Starlink

American Airlines may bring back seatback screens while exploring Starlink WiFi, signaling a shift toward enhanced inflight connectivity.

American Airlines' potential seatback screen reinvestment reflects competitive pressure to bundle premium connectivity and entertainment as differentiated revenue drivers rather than cost-cutting measures.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise-abatement procedures require pilots to navigate complex terrain and Class B airspace constraints that demand heightened situational awareness during departures.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke AFB's air show demonstrates continued public engagement with military aviation operations and serves as a recruitment and community relations tool for active-duty squadrons.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's synthetic aperture radar capability to penetrate cloud cover provides unprecedented Earth observation data for volcanic monitoring and natural disaster preparedness in regions with persistent weather challenges.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar enables all-weather Earth observation through cloud cover, expanding monitoring capabilities for volcanic and geological hazards critical to aviation flight planning.

See NASA →

▶
Safety Mar 27, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strikes kill helicopters because pilots systematically underestimate how quickly they approach wires and overestimate their reaction time to avoid them.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

NTSB Issues Preliminary Report on Hudson River Ditching

Investigators say an instructional flight ended after a loss of oil pressure and total engine failure.

Oil pressure loss leading to complete engine failure highlights critical maintenance or design vulnerabilities that warrant immediate fleet-wide inspection protocols.

See AVweb →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

How Premium Economy Has Changed Over The Last 10 Years

The transformation of premium economy from a niche offering to a central pillar of airline strategy has reshaped how passengers travel.

Premium economy's evolution from niche to core offering has become airlines' primary margin driver as they optimize cabin configurations between economy and business.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Tech and Science Bound for Low Earth Orbit on Commercial Launch

Technology and science demonstrations, supported by various NASA industry collaborations and agency developments, are set to launch to low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the company’s Transporter-16 commercial rideshare mission. These demonstrations will test thermal...

NASA's shift toward commercial rideshare missions reduces launch costs while accelerating technology validation cycles for aerospace developers and suppliers.

See NASA →

Industry Mar 27, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-flight systems offers near-term capacity and emissions gains while emerging technologies mature for longer-term sustainability.

See MDPI →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

House Transportation Chair Sam Graves To Retire

Graves helped shape FAA reauthorization, BasicMed and several other general aviation measures.

Loss of a key congressional advocate for general aviation could slow momentum on pending FAA modernization and pilot certification reforms.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Names Scientists to Support Lunar South Pole Science

NASA has selected 10 participating scientists to help shape a science plan for astronauts to complete on the lunar surface under the Artemis program – including deploying scientific instruments, making critical observations of the landing site, and collecting Moon rocks. “Congratulations to the...

NASA's scientist selection for Artemis lunar missions signals that surface operations will prioritize rigorous scientific methodology alongside exploration, requiring aviation and spacecraft teams to accommodate specialized equipment and extended surface protocols.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Three killed in Hawaii tour helicopter crash on Kauai

Three people died and two others were injured after an Airborne Aviation tour helicopter crashed on the afternoon…...

Tour helicopter operators face mounting liability exposure as accident investigations increasingly scrutinize maintenance records and pilot experience in scenic flight operations.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Michael Guzman

Listen to this audio excerpt from Michael Guzman, Artemis II main propulsion systems engineer: A clue to what Mike Guzman, main propulsion systems engineer at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, loves most can be found in the signature of his work email: a complex string of equations for rocket...

NASA's Artemis propulsion engineers like Guzman are critical to lunar mission success, as main propulsion systems require sophisticated mathematical modeling and expertise rarely found outside aerospace.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

SES meoSphere will be core to new MEO/LEO inflight connectivity

WASHINGTON D.C. — Rapidly putting the wheels in motion to launch a next-generation Medium Earth Orbit satellite network, dubbed meoSphere, SES intends to pair the new service with Low Earth Orbit satcom to power multi-orbit MEO/LEO inflight connectivity for airlines, company CEO Adel Al-Saleh...

SES's hybrid MEO/LEO meoSphere architecture addresses aviation's latency and capacity constraints by combining medium and low Earth orbit satellites for superior inflight connectivity performance.

See Runway Girl Network →

Drones Mar 27, 2026

This firmware update makes Avata 360 ready for real flying

The new DJI Avata 360 drone is arriving with a feature-rich day-one firmware update that significantly expands what this immersive FPV drone can do right out of the box. The initial firmware rollout, headlined by v01.00.0100 for the aircraft, arrives alongside updates for the entire ecosystem,...

DJI's day-one firmware update for Avata 360 expands FPV drone capabilities immediately upon release, reducing time-to-productivity for commercial and recreational operators.

See DroneDJ →

Military Mar 27, 2026

The World’s Largest Air Forces By Number Of Pilots

Which air force has the deepest roster?

Pilot supply depth correlates with force sustainability and operational readiness beyond aircraft quantity, revealing strategic advantages in extended operations and training capacity.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

India’s Bellatrix raises $20 million following overseas expansion drive

India-based Bellatrix Aerospace announced March 27 it has raised $20 million to ramp up production of its satellite propulsion systems after securing its first large commercial customer outside the country.

Bellatrix's overseas customer acquisition validates Indian satellite propulsion technology in global markets, signaling emerging aerospace manufacturing competitiveness beyond domestic space agencies.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NextSTEP-3 E: Network Extension for User Continuity and Sustainability (NEXUS) Ka-Band Backward-Compatible Relay Broad

Synopsis | 03/23/26 https://sam.gov/workspace/contract/opp/e33cd0cc61064a6497a55fca8e9b30c6/view NASA intends to release a BAA under Next Space Technologies for Exploration Partnerships (NextSTEP-3), Appendix E, for Project NEXUS, Ka-band Backward- Compatible Relay. As the aging Tracking and Data...

NASA's NEXUS project aims to modernize satellite relay infrastructure with Ka-band technology while maintaining backward compatibility with legacy tracking systems, enabling continuous space mission support beyond aging infrastructure retirement.

See NASA →

Military Mar 27, 2026

The 5 Highest Altitude Reconnaissance Aircraft Currently Operated By The United States

These aircraft offer long-range capabilities, a factor that pairs well with their high-altitude flying.

High-altitude reconnaissance platforms provide dual operational advantages through extended dwell time and reduced detection risk, making them critical for intelligence gathering missions.

See Simple Flying →

Drones Mar 27, 2026

DJI Fly update adds support for new Avata 360 drone

The DJI Fly app has officially been updated to version 1.20.0, adding support for the newly launched Avata 360 drone. The update also confirms Avata 360’s compatibility with the DJI RC 2, DJI Goggles 3, and DJI Goggles N3. more…...

DJI's ecosystem expansion through the Avata 360 integration demonstrates strategic cross-compatibility that reduces barriers to adoption for existing goggle and controller users.

See DroneDJ →

Commercial Mar 27, 2026

The $2B blind spot: Why ‘good enough’ is killing airline profitability

AeroTime columnist Ann Cederhall is an instructor with IATA on Airline Distribution Strategy and with Aeroclass on Airline Retailing, Ann is a frequent speaker and…...

Airlines' acceptance of suboptimal distribution and retailing practices costs the industry $2B annually, suggesting that operational excellence beyond flight operations directly impacts bottom-line profitability.

See AeroTime Hub →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

Tennessee Airport Boards Face Possible State Takeover

Senate panel advances measure to replace existing airport authority boards with state appointees.

State control of local airport boards could standardize operations but risks reducing community input in infrastructure decisions affecting regional aviation economies.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Artemis II Crew’s Suits

The Orion Crew Survival System suits that Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialist Christina Koch from NASA, and Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen from the CSA (Canadian Space Agency) will wear on the Artemis II test flight are seen in the suit-up room of the Neil A....

Advanced spacesuit design for lunar missions demonstrates critical life-support innovations applicable to high-altitude aviation emergency systems.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA Warns Of Stolen Aircraft Parts Threatening Global Flights

The agency has been alerted to a compromised shipment of more than 600 parts from four engine families.

Compromised engine parts from multiple manufacturers indicate a supply chain vulnerability affecting multiple aircraft types simultaneously, requiring urgent verification of recent maintenance records.
